coredata.dc:description: In this work, flower-like Bi2S3 was prepared by hydrothermal method using different solvents, and materials with better performance were selected. Graphene quantum dots (GQD, which is rich in specific amino groups) was prepared by aqueous phase fusion technology. Ultimately, a straightforward one-step hydrothermal process was used to successfully manufacture composite materials in order to build GQD(4)/Bi2S3 heterostructures. The study results have shown that the H2 production efficiency of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 is the best, and its photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency within 3 h is 5.92 times that of GQD(4) and 3.03 times that of Bi2S3. The H2O2 production and tetracycline (TC) degradation efficiency of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 are the best, while the methylene blue (MB) degradation efficiency of 1% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 is relatively better. The research results show that as a perfect combination of GQD(4)/Bi2S3 heterostructures, it offers a productive plan for eliminating organic pollutants in water and producing clean oxidant and hydrogen energy.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot @ Flower-like Bi2S3 composites for photodegradation of organic pollutants combined photosynthesis of H2 and H2O2 from treated water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, flower-like Bi2S3 was prepared by hydrothermal method using different solvents, and materials with better performance were selected. Graphene quantum dots (GQD, which is rich in specific amino groups) was prepared by aqueous phase fusion technology. Ultimately, a straightforward one-step hydrothermal process was used to successfully manufacture composite materials in order to build GQD(4)/Bi2S3 heterostructures. The study results have shown that the H2 production efficiency of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 is the best, and its photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency within 3 h is 5.92 times that of GQD(4) and 3.03 times that of Bi2S3. The H2O2 production and tetracycline (TC) degradation efficiency of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 are the best, while the methylene blue (MB) degradation efficiency of 1% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 is relatively better. The research results show that as a perfect combination of GQD(4)/Bi2S3 heterostructures, it offers a productive plan for eliminating organic pollutants in water and producing clean oxidant and hydrogen energy.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic reactions are one of the best research avenues available for creating green energy and environmental treatment. Photocatalytic hydrogen production and pollutant degradation is an environmentally friendly and sustainable energy conversion technology that can help solve the growing energy crisis and environmental problems [1–3]. A critical phase in the process of photocatalytic reactions is the selection of photocatalysts. The semiconductor photocatalyst produces photoelectron-hole pairs in response to light stimulation, which then migrate to the catalyst's surface to participate in redox processes [4–6]. Mixing rapidly of some photogenerated electrons and holes lead to reduced photocatalytic efficiency. Through metal ion doping, metal deposition, supported catalysts, morphological control, and heterojunction assembly, the catalyst's photocatalytic activity was increased [7–11].

Bi2S3 is a bismuth-based semiconductor photocatalyst with a narrow band gap (1.3–1.7 eV), low solubility, non-toxic, and good chemical stability, because of its low valence band potential energy and high conduction band potential energy, Bi2S3 has good visible light absorption and photocatalytic activity, and it can absorb a large part of visible light up to 800 nm [12–15]. Nevertheless, because of the substantial charge recombination in Bi2S3 alone, which prevents photogenerated electrons and holes from separating, its photocatalytic activity is generally lower than expected. Therefore, researchers have adopted various methods to enhance Bi2S3 materials' photocatalytic activity. First, prepare materials with specific structures, such as nanorods [16], sea urchin spheres [17], nanowires [18], microspheres [19], and flower shaped [20]. Three-dimensional (3D) flower-shaped structures composed of two-dimensional nanosheets have several advantages over other morphologies, including good conductivity, high specific surface area, minimal aggregation qualities, and interconnected porous channels [21]. Secondly, building heterojunction hybrid materials such as Bi2S3/BiOI [15,22], Bi2S3/Bi4O5Br2 [23], and Bi2S3/g-C3N4 [24] by coupling with different semiconductors can greatly improve charge separation and transfer at different parts of the heterojunction, thereby improving light conversion efficiency.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) refer to an emerging carbon fluorescent material with graphene sheets within 100 nm in size and less than 10 layers. Because of the functional groups at the edges, they are easily diffused in water, have a large surface mass ratio, and are chemically and physically stable. Its modified band gap, enhanced visible light trapping, faster charge transfer, and enhanced photogenerated carrier separation can significantly boost photocatalytic performance [25]. GQD has constructed heterogenous junction hybrid materials with different semiconductors for different applications, such as high-performance electrode materials [26,27], electrocatalysis [28], photocatalysis [29]. Recently, we had successfully synthesized the four nitropyrenes with different nitration degree, such as mononitropyrene, dinitropyrene, trinitropyrene and tetranitropyrene, and reasonably constructed the corresponding graphite phase quantum dots [30]. Subsequently, the relationship between the structure and photocatalytic activity of different intermediates for preparing GQD was systematically investigated, the degree of polymerization and lateral size of GQDs prepared with different intermediates have a significant impact on their photocatalytic properties. We found that GQD (4) had the best photocatalytic efficiency among the four GQDs. On the basis of our previous work, we envision compounding the superior properties of both materials in this work, flower-like Bi2S3 was prepared by hydrothermal method using different solvents, and GQD(4), which is uniform in size and enriched with specific amino groups, was prepared by aqueous phase fusion technique. Finally, GQD(4)/Bi2S3 multiphase catalysts were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Experiments on photocatalytic hydrogen production, hydrogen peroxide generation and pollutant degradation revealed that GQD(4)/Bi2S3 has superior visible photocatalytic performance than Bi2S3 and GQD(4).

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (purity >99%) came from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Thiourea (purity >99%) from Shanghai McLean Biotechnology Co., polyvinylpyrrolidone from Shanghai McLean Biotechnology Co., glycol (AR, purity >99%) is purchased from Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co., The solvents used in this study without further purification. Deionized water was used in the experiments.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Synthesis of Bi2S3 with two solvents in different ratios

ce:section.ce:para:
Dissolve 0.49 g (1.01 mmol) bismuth pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) in 30 mL distilled water (distilled water : Ethylene glycol (EG) = 2:1, distilled water : ethylene glycol (EG) = 1:1, ethylene glycol (EG)), and stir at room temperature for 2 h at a constant speed. Then added 0.60 g (7.88 mmol) thiourea (H2NCSNH2) and stirred for 30 min. Then 0.20 g polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was added and stirred for 2 h to obtain a uniform yellow solution. The mixture was moved to stainless steel autoclave and burned for 12 h at 180 °C. After the reactor was cooled, centrifuged to obtain black precipitation, and then washed three times each with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol to thoroughly clean it. 1.51 g black powder is obtained with a freeze-dryer and grinding, which is Bi2S3. Bi2S3 prepared by two solvents in different ratios are denoted as Bi2S3 (H2O), Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1), Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1), Bi2S3 (EG).
(2)
Preparation of GQD

2.00 g (9.89 mmol, purity >98%) pyrene was weighed into a flask, then 200 mL concentrated nitric acid was added to it and stirred at 100 °C for 16 h. The solution was washed to neutral after cooling with distilled water, dried at 60 °C and ground to obtain 3.45 g (90% yield) of yellow tetranitropyrene compound. 4 mmol of tetranitropyrene compound was weighed, and 220.60 mL distilled water and 19.40 mL concentrated ammonia water (1.20 M) were added to the compound, and ultrasonic was carried out in an ice bath for 2 h. The mixture was moved to a high pressure reactor and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. To extract the insoluble carbon, the mixture was run through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. About 220 mL of water was steamed using a rotary evaporator at 80 °C. Dialyzing the residual solution continues until the unfused small molecules are eliminated and the dialysis water turns colorless. The purified solution dried at 80 °C and further ground to obtain the black product, namely graphene quantum dots (GQD(4) in Ref. [30].
(3)
Preparation of GQD@Bi2S3 composites

0.49 g (1.01 mmol) bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) was dissolved in 30 mL mixed solution (H2O:EG = 2:1). After it was completely dissolved, different proportions of as-prepared GQD were added, and the mixture was mixed for 2 h at a constant speed. Then added 0.60 g (7.88 mmol) thiourea (H2NCSNH2) and stirred for 30 min. Then 0.20 g polyethylpyrrolidone (PVP) was added and stirred for 2 h to obtain a uniform black solution. Solvent heat at 180 °C for 12 h. Then centrifuged the solution at 8000 rpm to obtain black precipitation, and then washed three times each with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol to thoroughly clean it. After freeze-dryer and grinding, black powder with 90% yield is obtained, which is GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite material in Fig. 1 .

The composite's crystal structure was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (D8) with Cu-Kα radiation. The samples' microstructures were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Talos F200X). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD) using Al Kα radiation was used to obtain the surface chemical properties of the material. The UV–visible diffuse reflection spectrum (DRS) was obtained on the U–3900H UV visible spectrophotometer from Hitachi, Japan. The absorbance spectrum of the samples were tested with an UV-1800 instrument made by Shanghai Elite Technology Instrument Co., LTD. PL spectrum were collected using an F-7100 fluorescence spectrometer from Hitachi, Japan. The EISs of the samples were tested with the CHI660E instrument of Shanghai Chenhua Company. BET was performed using 3H–2000PS2 instrument from Beijing Best Company, with the following parameters: above 0.0005 (m2/g) (specific surface area); 2 nm–500 nm (mesopore and macroporous analysis); 0.35 nm−2 nm (microporous routine analysis). FTIR was tested using a Nicolet iS50 instrument from Thermo Fly. The Fourier digital nuclear magnetic resonance spectrum is measured with a NMR spectrometer, the Avance 400 MHz model instrument of Bruker, Germany, with a sensitivity of 367 S/N 1HNMR,0.1 EB 263S/N13CNMR. The molecular weight determination of the sample was tested by using the Q-Exactive model HRMS instrument of Thermo Scientific in the United States.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic hydrogen production from treated water
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation process of photocatalytic performance of aquatic hydrogen by different photocatalysts is as follows. Weigh 0.01 g catalyst and put it in 40 mL aqueous solution containing isopropyl alcohol, the proportion of isopropyl alcohol is 5%, ultrasonic 10 min to make the photocatalyst fully dispersed in the solution. The quartz tube containing catalyst and solution is sealed with a rubber plug and sealed with a sealing film. A 600 W Xenon lamp (visible light) was selected as the light source. The reactor's air was released and the agitator was activated prior to the light being switched on. After repeated readings by drainage and gas collection, the light was turned on and the reaction began. The change in value was recorded by reading it every 1 h. The reaction lasted for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2O2 production from treated water
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the photocatalytic performance of aquatic H2O2 using different photocatalysts, the following process is employed: 0.04 g of the photocatalyst is ultrasonically separated in 50 mL of water and stirred for 0.5 h in the absence of light to ensure full contact between the solution and air. Light for the photocatalytic is provided by a 600 W Xenon lamp. Throughout the illumination period, 2 mL solution is sampled per hour, and took out catalyst by filter. The concentration of H2O2 is determined through iodometry.1 mL collected solution is putted into 5 mL tube. Subsequently, 1 mL of 0.1 mol-L−1 aqueous potassium hydrogen phthalate (C8H5KO4) and 1 mL of 0.4 mol-L−1 aqueous potassium iodide (KI) were added [31]. Using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, the solution's absorbance at 350 nm was determined after 30 min. Using the acquired absorbance value as a foundation, the H2O2 yield is determined and computed by standard curve of H2O2 amount vis absorbance (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information).

The performance evaluation process of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants using different photocatalysts is as follows. Disperse 0.01 g of the photocatalyst in a 40 mL solution containing pollutants. Start magnetic stirring and stir uniformly for 0.5 h in the absence of light to achieve adsorption equilibrium in the photocatalytic system. The photocatalytic process should then be carried out with a 600 W xenon lamp and sample 4 mL of the solution every 1 h. Filter the extracted reaction solution and use UV–Vis spectrophotometer to measure the filtrate. Determine the rate of degradation by utilizing the absorbance at the wavelength of maximum absorption.

The performance evaluation process of different photocatalysts for photocatalytic adsorption of pollutants is as follows. The 40 mL MB solution was used to disperse the 0.01 g photocatalyst and magnetic stirrer was started to make the catalyst fully contact with MB, So as to complete the catalyst's purification and adsorption of pollutants.

The structural characterization of the GQD (4) was provided in our previous paper [30]. The crystal structure and X-ray diffraction characterization of Bi2S3 prepared with two solvents in different ratios as displayed in Fig. 2 a. The prepared sample's strong and sharp diffraction peak is evident from the figure, indicating that it has good crystallinity and is in line with the pure intersection structure standard card (JCPDS No. 17–0320) [32]. 15.74°, 33.94°, 45.53°, 54.67° of Bi2S3 correspond to (020), (311), (002), and (351) crystal planes. The diffraction peaks of four kinds of Bi2S3 were compared, and the crystal plane diffraction peaks of Bi2S3 prepared with water as solvent were significantly stronger and sharper than those of the other three kinds of Bi2S3, indicating higher crystallinity. The XRD patterns of pure GQD, Bi2S3 and composite catalysts with different composite proportions are displayed in Fig. 2b. The figure illustrates that after doping GQD(4) in Bi2S3, with the increase of doping proportion, the diffraction peak becomes sharper and the intensity increases. Compared with all composite catalysts. It indicates that 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 has higher crystallinity. Furthermore, no additional diffraction peaks were seen in GQD(4)/Bi2S3 sample, indicating that its purity was good.

Bi2S3 samples made using two solvents in different ratios had their surface morphologies examined, and the results are displayed in Fig. 3 a–d.The morphologies of four kinds of Bi2S3 are all flowerlike. In addition, it was found that the solvent had an important effect on the final morphology of Bi2S3. Fig. 3e is the TEM image of pure Bi2S3 prepared with H2O : EG = 2:1 as solvent. It can be seen from the figure that the material is composed of nanorods, whose surface is smooth and uniform, and whose diameter is about 20 nm. The electron diffraction pattern in the illustration shows that the prepared sample has an almost perfect crystalline structure. Fig. 3f–i shows TEM images of GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite photocatalysts of different proportions. GQD(4) is evenly dispersed on the surface of Bi2S3, indicating that GQD(4) has been successfully loaded on Bi2S3. Fig. 3j is the EDS element mapping of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite. The analysis results of the elemental mapping diagram show that the prepared sample contains C, N, Bi, S and O element may be incorporated into O element because the sample was prepared in air. These elements were uniformly dispersed in the material, further confirming the success of GQD/Bi2S3 preparation.

XPS was used to further analyze the chemical composition and oxidation state of the material, and the results are displayed in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the full XPS spectrum of pure GQD(4), Bi2S3 and 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3, and the elements of Bi, S, C and O were detected in the 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3, which proves that the composites were successfully prepared. Fig. 4b shows the XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Bi 4f and S 2p. The peak aroud 284 eV in C 1s is considered to be the C–C bond of graphene sp2 hybridization, the characteristic peak at 285.4 eV corresponds to C–N, the pyridine nitrogen introduced at the edge of the quantum dots after the reaction and the characteristic peak at 287.60 eV corresponds to the C–OH derived from the use of ammonia aqueous in the GQD (4) preparation. Peaks of the approximate positions were also found in the C 1s spectrum of 2% GQD (4)/Bi2S3 [33,34].

As the GQD(4) was prepared in ammonia solution, the edge sites are functionalized by NH2, the characteristic peak at 397.6 eV is attributed to N–C, the pyridine nitrogen introduced at the edge of the quantum dots after the reaction. And the characteristic peak at 400.1 eV is attributed to –NH2. This also gives an explanation for the N source in the composite material [35,36].

In the O 1s spectra, it is seen that the source of oxygen is mainly the unreacted –NO2 and the introduced –OH. Bi2S3 has two sets of spin-orbit coupling peaks at 157.2, 157.3, 162.4, and 162.7 eV, which are attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, indicating the presence of Bi3+ in Bi2S3. Two peaks at 159.8 and 161.1 eV correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 due to the spin-orbit separation of the S element, indicating the presence of S2− in Bi2S3,and related peaks were also found at similar positions in the composites, demonstrating the presence of Bi2S3 in nanocomposites [37,38].The valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VBXPS) in Fig. S2 was conducted to measure the position of valence bands (VB) for Bi2S3 and GQD(4), which were 1.32 eV and 0.98 eV, respectively.

The UV–Vis spectra of four pure Bi2S3 are displayed in Fig. 5 a. The image shows that despite the material's lack of a clear absorption peak, Its outstanding light response across the visible spectrum suggests that Bi2S3 can fully utilize visible light. Fig. 5b shows the UV–Visible spectra of Bi2S3, GQD prepared with H2O : EG = 2:1 as solvent and the composite catalysts of different proportions. The image illustrates how the content of GQD in Bi2S3 has increased, its visible light absorption capacity is gradually enhanced, mainly because Bi2S3 forms heterojunction with GQD, which encouraged photogenic charge carrier separation. The fluorescence spectra were examined to enhance comprehension of the recombination probability of photogenerated electrons and holes in the produced photocatalyst. The optical characteristics of materials were examined experimentally in order to have a better knowledge of photocatalytic performance and mechanisms.UV/Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS), which revealed that the band gaps of GQD(4) and Bi2S3 were 1.35 eV and 1.58 eV, respectively. (Fig. S3 in Supporting Information) [39].

The PL spectra acquired by stimulating four pure Bi2S3 samples at a 500 nm excitation wavelength are displayed in Fig. 5c. The figure shows that four Bi2S3 have a peak around 755 nm. The peak position of Bi2S3 prepared with different solvents is roughly the same, but the peak intensity is different. Fig. 5d shows the PL spectra obtained by GQD at 510 nm excitation wavelength. The figure shows that 568 nm is the wavelength at which the maximum fluorescence intensity occurs. Fig. 5e shows the PL spectra of 450 nm as excitation wavelength for composite photocatalysts with different composite proportions. The figure indicates that their maximum wavelength of fluorescence intensity is 668 nm. Although the positions of absorption peaks are similar, intensifies are different, among which 2% GQD/Bi2S3 composite has the lowest fluorescence intensity.

It is known that low photocatalytic recombination rate of photoelectrons and holes is indicated by weak fluorescence intensity, and this is favorable for increasing photocatalytic activity. Consequently, the separation of photoinduced and photogenerated electrons from holes can be efficiently accelerated by 2% GQD/Bi2S3, which greatly improves the photocatalytic ability of Bi2S3. The sample's electrochemical impedance was measured, and the outcomes are displayed in Fig. 5f. The arc radius of the 2% GQD/Bi2S3 sample is smaller than that of the pure Bi2S3 and GQD sample, as the figure makes evident, and its arc radius is the smallest, suggesting that doping GQD can lower photogenerated electron migration resistance and speed up photogenerated electron transmission [25]. In addition, charge migration and separation are facilitated by small charge transfer resistance.

The specific surface area and pore volume of the collected samples were determined by analyzing the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm curves (Fig. S4 in Supporting Information) and relevant data were listed in Table S1 (Supporting Information). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of all four are typical type curves with H3 type hysteresis lines, indicating the presence of mesoporous structure [14]. The pore volume and pore size of Bi2S3 produced with H2O : EG = 2:1 are the greatest, while the surface area progressively increased with the addition of ethylene glycol. To enhance the photocatalytic activity, more active areas for the adsorption and reaction can frequently be found with a higher specific surface area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance test of four kinds of Bi2S3
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate how various solvents affect the characteristics of photocatalytic materials, photocatalytic materials were used for water cracking to produce H2, H2O2, MB and TC, and the photocatalytic activities of Bi2S3 prepared by these two solvents in different ratios were compared.

Fig. S5 displays the results of an evaluation of the hydrogen production activities of four Bi2S3 photocatalytic water splitting processes conducted in visible light. As the image illustrates, the photocatalytic activity of Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) is relatively good. In the presence of visible light, the efficiency of hydrogen generation within 3 h is as high as 286.72 μmol g−1•h−1, and the photocatalytic activity of Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1) and Bi2S3 (H2O) is second. The photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency of Bi2S3 (EG) was 198.14 μmol g−1•h−1 and 169.03 μmol g−1•h−1, respectively. Under the same test conditions, the hydrogen production efficiency of Bi2S3 (EG) was the lowest, which was 165.72 μmol g−1•h−1. After that, the four Bi2S3 were repeated for 9 h under the same conditions to consider their stability. H2 is generated continuously after cycling, and the reduction amount is small, indicating that Bi2S3 has good stability. Over time, hydrogen production decreased slightly due to the loss of catalysts.

The results of producing H2O2 under visible light irradiation are shown in Fig. 6 in order to evaluate its photocatalytic activity. Fig. 6a shows the relationship between photocatalytic H2O2 production of four kinds of Bi2S3. The figure illustrates the comparatively good photocatalytic activity of Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1), and the photocatalytic activity of Bi2S3 (EG) and Bi2S3 (H2O) is low under visible light irradiation within 6 h. Within 6 h, 98.72 μM H2O2 and 83.73 μM H2O2 were produced under visible light irradiation, respectively. The Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1) material showed the worst photocatalytic activity, producing 54.21 μm H2O2. To find out how the sacrificial agent, isopropyl alcohol, affects the photocatalytic activity, isopropyl alcohol (v/v = 5%) was added to the aqueous solution under the same conditions as the sacrificial agent to conduct the photocatalytic activity test.

In Fig. 6b, the test results are displayed. As the image illustrates, the addition of sacrificial agent can improve the photocatalytic effect. The photocatalytic production of H2O2 by Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) under visible light irradiation can reach 125.32 μM, which is 1.1 times of the photocatalytic rate without sacrificial agent. The photocatalytic activity of Bi2S3 (EG) and Bi2S3 (H2O) was the second, and they produced 113.24 and 106.91 μM H2O2 under visible light irradiation within 6 h, respectively. Notably, after three consecutive 18 h cycles, the H2O2 formation activity did not decrease significantly, indicating that the prepared Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) sample has good recyclability and physicochemical stability. To investigate how various pH levels affect the photocatalytic activity, the pH values of the sacrificant added to the solution were changed. The pH values of H2SO4 and NaOH were adjusted to 4 and 10 respectively(Fig. 6c-d), and the photocatalytic performance was tested under the same test conditions. Its H2O2 formation rate is significantly enhanced, Bi2S3 (H2O), Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1), Bi2S3(H2O : EG = 1:1), and Bi2S3 (EG) produced 173.12, 243.13, 194.52, and 151.30 μM H2O2, respectively, within 6 h of visible light irradiation. When pH = 4, its H2O2 generation efficiency is also improved, and its photocatalytic activity is between pH = 7 and pH = 10.

To gain additional insight into the photocatalytic materials' effectiveness, methylene blue (MB) and tetracycline (TC) were photocatalytic degradation experiments under the irradiation of visible light using these four Bi2S3. Fig. 7 displays the test findings. Fig. 7a displays the MB curve of photocatalytic degradation including dark adsorption. Within 30 min of dark adsorption, Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) has the best adsorption rate for MB, which is 13.5%, followed by Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1) and Bi2S3 (EG), whose adsorption rates are 8.8% and 2.0%, respectively. The adsorption capacity of Bi2S3 (H2O) was the least, and the adsorption rate was 0.21%. Following the reduction of the adsorption capacity, Fig. 7b illustrates the photocatalytic degradation. The chart shows that the rate of degradation of Bi2S3 (H2O) within 4 h is 12.63%, and that of Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) within 4 h is 5.92%. The degradation rate of Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1) was 5.84% within 4 h. The photocatalytic degradation performance of Bi2S3 (EG) was the worst, with a degradation rate of 4.70% within 4 h. According to the above data, Bi2S3 has poor degradation effect on MB. Fig. 7c shows the TC curve of photocatalytic degradation including dark adsorption. Within 30 min of dark adsorption, Bi2S3(EG)has the best adsorption rate of 32.44%, followed by Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1) and Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1),and Bi2S3 (H2O) has the worst adsorption capacity. The adsorption rate was 15.12%.

Fig. 7d shows the photocatalytic degradation after removal of adsorption capacity. Bi2S3(EG) has the best degradation effect within 4 h with a degradation rate of 38.84%, followed by Bi2S3(EG) and Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) within 4 h Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1) was worst, with a degradation rate of 11.81% within 4 h. The reusability and stability of photocatalytic degradation of MB and TC were evaluated by cyclic degradation experiments. After 12 h continuous testing, the sample still showed good stability, and the outcomes were displayed in Fig. 7e and f. Bi2S3 (H2O) and Bi2S3 (EG) are stable and can be used for the degradation of MB and TC pollutants.

To further evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the MB and TC solutions, the H2O2 was produced along with the degradation, and the results are shown in Fig. S6. Fig. S6a shows the amount of H2O2 produced to degrade MB. Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) has the best photocatalytic activity to produce H2O2 while grading MB, which produces 1.72 μM H2O2 within 4 h under visible light irradiation. Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 1:1), Bi2S3 (EG) produced the second H2O2 content, and Bi2S3 (H2O) produced the least H2O2. Fig. S6b shows the map of H2O2 content generated during TC degradation. It can be seen that Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) produces the best photocatalytic activity of H2O2 while grading TC, which produces a total of 45.73 μM H2O2 within 4 h under visible light irradiation. Bi2S3 (H2O: EG = 1:1), Bi2S3 (EG) produced the second H2O2 content, and Bi2S3 (H2O) produced the least H2O2,31.72 μM. By comprehensive comparison, Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) produced much more H2O2 in TC degradation than H2O2 in MB degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance test of GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 displays the results of an evaluation of the photocatalytic hydrogen generation activity of water cracking employing various composite ratios GQD(4)/Bi2S3 under the influence of visible light. When pure Bi2S3 and GQD(4) are used alone for photocatalytic hydrogen production, H2 production is small, because pure Bi2S3 and GQD(4) show low photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity due to the easy recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. It is important to note that when GQD(4) when the load is only 0.5%, H2 generation rate increased to 484.71 μmol g−1•h−1. With the further increase of GQD(4) load, the H2 precipitation rate also increases. When GQD(4) load was 2%, the H2 generation rate is 869.12 μmol g−1•h−1, which is about 3.03 times of pure Bi2S3. It is further proved that the presence of GQD(4) can efficiently reduce the rate at which Bi2S3 photogenerated carriers recombine, and enhance the photocatalytic activity of Bi2S3. Table 1 summarizes some catalysts similar to GQD(4)/Bi2S3, and compares their hydrogen production efficiency. It is found that the photocatalytic performance of the prepared GQD(4)/Bi2S3 catalyst is relatively good, this suggests that the catalyst has a promising future in the catalytic hydrogen production industry.

The ability of the produced sample to produce H2O2 when exposed to visible light was investigated in order to evaluate its photocatalytic activity even more. Fig. 9 presents the findings, the production of H2O2 gradually increases with the extension of reaction time. GQD(4) and Bi2S3 alone have poor photocatalytic activity. Doping GQD(4) to modify Bi2S3, the increase of H2O2 content depends on the dose of GQD(4). Fig. 9a illustrates that 2% is the ideal GQD(4) addition amount, and 415.42 μM H2O2 is produced within 6 h, which is 1.70 and 3.50 times of H2O2 produced by GQD(4) and Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1). The synthesis of H2O2 will gradually rise as the content of GQD(4) increases, which is because GQD(4) has a good effect on enhancing the photocatalytic activity of Bi2S3. To investigate the impact of isopropyl alcohol, the sacrificial agent, on the photocatalytic activity, isopropyl alcohol (v/v = 5%) was added to the aqueous solution under the same conditions as the sacrificial agent to conduct the photocatalytic activity test. In Fig. 9b, the test results are displayed. H2O2 produced by 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite photocatalysis under visible light irradiation can reach 475.52 μM, and the photocatalytic rate is 1.1 times of that without sacrificant added. The photocatalytic activity of 1.5% and 2.0% doping was the second, and 409.23 and 325.71 μM H2O2 were produced under visible light irradiation within 6 h, respectively. As a result, sacrificial agents can be added to greatly increase photocatalytic efficiency and prevent photogenerated electrons and holes from recombining. Fig. 9c shows the UV–Vis spectrum of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 under visible light irradiation. Because 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 has good photocatalytic activity, three continuous reaction cycles of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 were carried out for practical application under the same conditions. After being removed from the reaction solution by centrifugation, the photocatalyst was washed with distilled water and dried at 60 °C. The outcomes are displayed in Fig. 9d, confirming the reusability of 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite photocatalyst. In the third run 18 h later, H2O2 production on 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 catalyst was close to 447.22 μM. In addition, during repeated experiments, H2O2 attenuation occurred on 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 photocatalyst, which may be the result of the loss of the middle spectral catalyst during the separation and washing process. To investigate how varying pH affects photocatalytic activity, the pH of the solution added with isopropyl alcohol was changed, and the pH of H2SO4 and NaOH were adjusted to 4 and 10 respectively. The photocatalytic performance was tested under the same test conditions, and the outcomes were displayed in Fig. 9e and f. It was found that when pH = 10, Its H2O2 formation rate is significantly enhanced, The H2O2 production rates of 0.5% GQD(4)/Bi2S3, 1% GQD(4)/Bi2S3, 1.5% GQD(4)/Bi2S3, 2% GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite photocatalysts under visible light irradiation are 408.12, 442.04, 482.11, 543.12 μmol g−1•h−1. When pH = 4, the H2O2 formation rate drops instead of rising.

To gain additional insight into the photocatalytic materials' efficiency, methylene blue (MB) and tetracycline (TC) were photocatalytic degradation experiments under the irradiation of visible light using these four composite photocatalysts of different proportions. The photocatalytic degradation MB curve, which incorporates dark adsorption, is depicted in Fig. 10 a (in Supporting Information). In the 30 min of dark adsorption, compared with other composite photocatalysts, 1% GQD/Bi2S3 has the best adsorption rate for MB, which is 16.12%, and 2% GQD/Bi2S3 has the least adsorption capacity, which is 5.03%. Fig. 10b shows photocatalytic degradation after removal of adsorption amount.The graphic illustrates that the rates of degradation of 0.5% GQD/Bi2S3, 1% GQD/Bi2S3, 1.5% GQD/Bi2S3 and 2% GQD/Bi2S3 were 17.52%, 17.43%, 20.63% and 21.71%, respectively. Fig. 10c shows the TC curve of photocatalytic degradation including dark adsorption. Within 30 min of dark adsorption, 2% GQD/Bi2S3 has the best adsorption rate of TC, and its adsorption efficiency is 57.42%, followed by 1.51% GQD/Bi2S3 and 1% GQD/Bi2S3. The adsorption capacity of 0.5% GQD/Bi2S3 was the worst, and the adsorption rate was 46.32%. Fig. 10d shows the photocatalytic degradation after removal of adsorption capacity. The figure shows that, with a degradation rate of 51.43%, 2% GQD/Bi2S3 has the best degradation impact while, with a degradation rate of 33.82% during 4 h, 1.5% GQD/Bi2S3 has the worst degradation effect. The reusability and stability of photocatalytic degradation of MB and TC were evaluated by cyclic degradation experiments. Because the performance of 2% GQD/Bi2S3 is relatively good, the sample still shows good stability after continuous testing for 12 h, and Fig. 10e and f displays the results. The figure indicates that 2% GQD/Bi2S3 photocatalytic efficiency is steady and can be applied to the degradation of MB and TC pollutants.

The photocatalytic performance of the catalyst was further evaluated by grading the MB and TC solutions. Its results are shown in Fig. S7. Fig. S7a shows the H2O2 curve generated by the composite photocatalyst for MB. Fig. S7b shows the H2O2 content generated by the composite photocatalyst to degrade TC. It can be seen that 2% GQD (4)/Bi2S3 produced the best photocatalytic activity of H2O2 while grading TC, which produced a total of 74.21 μM H2O2 within 4 h under visible light irradiation. The H2O2 content was followed by 1.5% GQD (4)/Bi2S3 1% GQD (4)/Bi2S3 and 0.5% GQD (4)/Bi2S3 with a yield of 67.33 μM. It is proved that the presence of GQD (4) increases the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes in Bi2S3.

In this paper, four kinds of flowered Bi2S3 were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method with different solvents. Bi2S3 (H2O : EG = 2:1) with good performance was selected from the four kinds of Bi2S3 through photocatalytic performance test, and then GQD(4)/Bi2S3 composite photocatalyst was prepared and validated using various characterization methods. The findings demonstrate that various solvents have distinct effects on the materials' photocatalytic activity. By comparing the photocatalytic activities of the photocatalysts mentioned above, it is found that the addition of GQD can improve the photocatalytic efficiency and effectively inhibit the photogenerated carrier recombination. We think that the development of this composite photocatalyst material will offer a straightforward and long-term plan for energy generation and water environmental treatment.
